بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمَٰنِ الرَّحِيمِ

السيّد أحمد شهاب الدِّين العَيْدَروس

رضي الله عنه

Kale Bawa

Hazrat Khwaja Shah Sayyid Ahmad Shihabuddin al-Aydarus

18 Ramaḍān 1298 — 14 August 1881  ·  Surat, Gujarat  ✦  d. at sea, 8 Rabīʿ al-Ākhir 1368 — 8 February 1949

A descendant of the Prophet ﷺ and a master of the Chishti–Nizami–Aydarusi order — scholar, spiritual guide, social reformer, a leading figure in India’s Khilafat and Non-Cooperation movements, and worked alongside Gandhi against British rule. He carried his spiritual mission across the ocean to South Africa and Botswana, and came to rest, by a captain's dream, in Mombasa.

His life⌄

  2. His parents & family
  3. Spiritual education and authorisation

Origins

Birth, ancestry & family

He was born in Surat, Gujarat on 18 Ramaḍān 1298 AH — 14 August 1881. Popularly known as Kale Bawa, "the saint clad in black," through an act of fearlessness recounted later in these pages, he was a descendant of the Prophet ﷺ, a spiritual luminary and master of the Chishti–Nizami–ʿAydarusi order, a respected scholar, a social reformer, and a leading Muslim figure in India's Khilafat and Non-Cooperation movements. He served as president of the Surat District Congress Committee, worked alongside Mahatma Gandhi in the Indian struggle against British rule, and later devoted himself fully to the spiritual formation and service of his followers.

His influence extended beyond India to South Africa and the Bechuanaland Protectorate, now Botswana. He passed away at sea while returning from South Africa to India, and was buried in Mombasa.

Kale Bawa belonged to the distinguished Bā ʿAlawī sayyids, a family tracing its ancestry through Imam Ḥusayn to the Prophet Muḥammad, peace and blessings be upon him and his family and companions.

From Tarim to Gujarat

An early ancestor, Shaykh ibn ʿAbd Allāh al-ʿAydarūs, migrated from Tarim to Gujarat in 958 AH (1551–1552). He settled in Ahmedabad and passed away there. He was not the first of the Bā ʿAlawī family to settle in India — they had been elsewhere in the subcontinent for centuries — but he was the first of the ʿAydarūs line to settle there. He was well-travelled and one of the foremost scholarly and spiritual authorities of his time, and also an entrepreneur in maritime transport and trade between India and Hadramawt. He wrote several sophisticated works on Sufi metaphysics, theology, devotional practice, poetry and spiritual discipline, including al-ʿIqd al-Nabawī as-Sirr as-Muṣṭafawī and Ḥaqāʾiq at-Tawḥīd.

His grandson, Shaykh al-Islām Imam Jamāl al-Dīn Muḥammad ibn ʿAbdullāh al-ʿAydarūs, was born in Tarim and settled in Surat. He became known as Ṣāḥib-e-Surat, "Master of Surat," and Mīr-e-ʿArab, "leader of the Arabs." His religious establishment and resting place are known to this day as Bade Edroos.

Ṣāḥib-e-Surat became the foremost ʿAlawī authority in all of Gujarat. He was generously patronised by the Mughal emperors, and even the political, commercial and social elite of Surat gathered around him as students and followers. His surviving work, Īḍāḥ Asrār ʿUlūm al-Muqarrabīn — published in English as Secrets of Those Drawn Near — presents a practical, socially engaged and moderate approach to Sufism, stressing the guarding of one's inner states and the spiritual art of transacting with God and His creation.

He passed away in Surat in 1031 AH (1621). One of his sons, ʿAbd al-Raḥmān as-Saqqāf, lived and passed away in Tarim, and it was through his descendants that the line leading to Kale Bawa continued. Later, ʿAbd al-Raḥmān’s great-grandson, ʿAlī Zayn al-ʿĀbidīn, travelled from Tarim to Surat to take up the spiritual leadership of the establishment founded by Ṣāḥib-e-Surat, winning the esteem of townspeople, rulers and nobles alike. Subsequent generations remained in Surat. It was within this established environment of scholarship, spiritual authority and public service that Kale Bawa was born.

Family

His parents & siblings

Kale Bawa was the eldest son of Hazrat Mawlānā Pīr Sayyid Muṣṭafā bin Saqqāf al-ʿAydarūs, known affectionately as Muṣṭafā Dādā. His mother was Kamrunnisha Begum.

Muṣṭafā Dādā was a scholar and Sufi master trained in both the outward and inward sciences. He received khilāfat (spiritual authorisation) from Hazrat Sayyid Rashīduddīn Chishtī, known as Nādir ʿAlī Shāh Bawa; Hazrat Sayyid Muḥammad Ḥusayn Shāhjahānpūrī; Hazrat Mirzā Bahādur Shāh; and, in the Rifāʿī order, Hazrat Sayyid Nūruddīn Rifāʿī Ṣāḥib. He was the founder of the Chishti ʿAydarūsī (Edrusi) order.

His arrival in the Transvaal Colony in 1902, in the region now forming part of South Africa's North West Province, is remembered as the beginning of a new era of God-consciousness among the Muslim families of the area. A natural educator, he organised the Thursday-night gatherings of remembrance, the Bara Tasbeeh (Zikrullah). Above all, he taught simplicity and humility. In 1926, he revisited South Africa. He lived to about eighty years, and was buried at Bade Edroos in Surat. His children were:

  • Hazrat Sayyid Aḥmad Shihābuddīn — Kale Bawa
  • Hazrat Sayyid ʿAbd al-Qādir al-ʿAydarūs — Majle Bawa
  • Hazrat Sayyid Muḥammad al-ʿAydarūs — Chote Bawa
  • Sayyida Munīra Begum

Initiation

Spiritual education & authorisation

Kale Bawa received khilāfat (spiritual authorisation) from:

  • His father, Hazrat Pīr Sayyid Muṣṭafā al-ʿAydarūs
  • Hazrat Sayyid Rashīduddīn Chishtī — Nādir Shāh Bawa
  • Hazrat Sayyid Muḥammad Ḥusayn Shāhjahānpūrī
  • Hazrat Mirzā Bahādur Shāh
  • Ṣūfī Ṣāḥib Lajporī — in the Sabri tradition

His spiritual identity joined the Indian Chishti inheritance to the Bā ʿAlawī ʿAydarūs traditions of Hadramawt and Gujarat. He became an important representative of the Chishti–Nizami–ʿAydarusi spiritual order.

His early connection with Southern Africa

Kale Bawa first visited South Africa in 1904. He left a lasting impression, particularly in the western Transvaal and the area now known as South Africa's North West Province. He was also among the early Sufi teachers who carried Islam to the Bechuanaland Protectorate, present-day Botswana.

The freedom movement

Kale Bawa and India's struggle

Kale Bawa was the most prominent Muslim figure in the public life of Surat. His role in the independence movement is not merely family tradition: it is recorded in colonial intelligence reports and discussed in later historical scholarship, including the work of the historian Douglas E. Haynes, who identifies him as a member of Surat's distinguished Edrus family, a family known for performing miracles.

He held a senior post as Superintendent in the Excise and Customs Department of the Bombay government. In 1920 he resigned from this secure and prestigious post in direct protest against British policy toward the Ottoman Caliphate, and threw himself into organising the Muslims of Surat. He soon became President of the Surat District Congress Committee, placing him in the formal leadership of the Khilafat and Non-Cooperation campaigns in Gujarat. His position was not ceremonial: he chaired major public meetings, introduced political resolutions, expressed his views through the press, and helped formulate the religious case for resistance to British rule.

  1. 1920 Resigns as Superintendent of Excise & Customs in protest at British policy toward the Ottoman Caliphate.
  2. 27–29 August 1920 · Ahmedabad At the Fourth Gujarat Political Conference, it is Kale Bawa who moves the major resolution condemning the harsh peace terms imposed on Ottoman Turkey. Immediately afterward, Gandhi rises to introduce his historic Non-Cooperation resolution.
  3. 6 October 1920 · Tilak Maidan, Surat Some ten thousand people assemble; Kale Bawa is elected chairman as Gandhi and the Ali brothers — Muhammad Ali and Shaukat Ali — speak on the Khilafat cause, non-cooperation, national schools, boycotting the colonial legislatures, and arbitration through panchayats.
  4. 19 April 1921 · Surat railway station When Gandhi arrives in Surat, Kale Bawa is the most important local leader to receive him — alongside Vallabhbhai Patel, later India's first deputy prime minister, who came from Ahmedabad, and Sarojini Naidu, who came from Bombay.
  5. Later in 1921 He is associated with a gathering of some six thousand addressed by Patel, on the goal of Swaraj, opposition to the Prince of Wales's visit, and support for Surat's non-cooperating municipality.
  6. January 1922 He objects when no Muslims are included in a Congress Working Committee session held in Surat, and opposes a proposed municipal restriction on cattle slaughter, fearing it would alienate Muslims and damage Hindu–Muslim cooperation. The proposal is withdrawn.

The sanctity of the Islamic holy lands and defending the Khilafat were binding Islamic responsibilities and therefore no Muslim can tolerate subjugation of either by any non-Muslim power.

From his letter to the Bombay Chronicle

Kale Bawa was an articulate public voice, and also presented his political and religious views through the press.

His stance combined active resistance with restraint. He did not advocate passivity, but he opposed uncontrolled violence and public disorder, seeking to preserve calm, communal harmony and the disciplined character of the movement. On at least one recorded occasion, Gandhi and him travelled together during a regional campaign of nationalist education, village mobilisation and public meetings. Yet he did not support every decision of the Congress leadership, and challenged it from within whenever he believed Muslim participation, religious sensitivities or communal fairness were being neglected.

The name

Kāle bāwā

During India's struggle for independence, Sayyid Aḥmad Shihābuddīn took a vow that he would wear nothing but black until India was free. Dressing in black was a recognised form of protest in those years, and he wore it as a constant declaration against British rule.

The title itself was born at a political gathering in the Gandhi Gardens of Surat. As the dignitaries sat upon the platform, a large snake appeared among them, causing alarm. Without hesitation, Sayyid Aḥmad threw his black shawl over the snake and held it down until the entire proceedings had concluded.

Among those who witnessed it was Sarojini Naidu — the poet and freedom fighter known across India as the "Nightingale of India." Moved by what she saw, she called out "Siyāh Posh!" — Persian for "the one robed in black." The name took root. In the local tongue it became Kale Bawa: kāle, black, and bāwā, an honorific for a saint or spiritual elder.

Passive resistance — and independence

When the British authorities retaliated against him and burned all the furniture in his home, he simply smiled, remaining committed to the principle of passive resistance. And when independence finally came in 1947, he lit candles throughout the entire house in celebration.

Discipline

His spiritual austerities

He withdrew from political life in 1942, turning thereafter to the spiritual nurturing of his devotees. For all his public standing, those closest to him knew him first as a man of intense inner discipline and resolute determination. He undertook long and arduous spiritual exertion, observing difficult fasts continuously over a period of three years, until his health deteriorated.

The journey through the jungle

One of the narratives concerns his young daughter, Sayeda Fatima Begum. She was about six years old when the family travelled, around 1940 or 1941, to visit the resting place of Hazrat Nādir Shāh Bawa at Bansda, near Bilimora in Gujarat. It was past midnight, and their horse carriage, driven by a man named Mirzā Sāheb, was crossing a thick jungle near the village of Rankuwa. Fatima Begum lay asleep on her father's lap.

Suddenly the horses began to neigh and refused to move further. Peering out, the travellers found their way blocked by four lions, ready to spring. The child awoke frightened and in tears, but Kale Bawa remained perfectly calm and gently patted her head. He asked Mirzā Sāheb and her to walk up to the lions and deliver his greeting: "Sayyid Aḥmad ʿAydarūs āp ko unkā salām bhejte hain" — "Sayyid Aḥmad ʿAydarūs sends you his salaams."

Fatima Begum was astounded, and wondered whether he truly expected them to do this. Mirzā Sāheb did not hesitate; he began walking toward the lions, the young girl following him, prodded by her father. The lions roared ferociously, their eyes glowing vividly in the dark. Yet as the two drew near, they sensed a spiritual protection settle over them, and their fear evaporated. Loudly they conveyed the saint's greeting. At once the lions fell silent. They stood completely still, then, with an almost royal dignity, bowed their heads in the direction of Kale Bawa and walked away into the dark. The child scurried back to her father, who smiled and patted her gently back to sleep. The horses moved on, and the journey continued.

The last mission

His mission across the seas

Kale Bawa's final visit to South Africa was in 1948. By then he was elderly and in fragile health, yet his energy for service was undiminished. He introduced the annual Urs (the commemoration) of Hazrat Khwāja Muʿīnuddīn Chishtī of Ajmer to the community there, entrusting its organisation to his disciple Goolam Rasool Dada. He worked tirelessly with the local Muslim communities, providing spiritual support and leadership, and pressed three things upon them: the education of women, cleanliness, and peace. It is said that even in his weakest state he would remain awake until 3 or 4 a.m. everyday to receive the people who had travelled long distances simply to see him and seek his advice, prayers or spiritual comfort.

Hordes of people gathered in Durban to bid him farewell when he departed for India in 1949. His younger brother, Hazrat Sayyid Muḥammad al-ʿAydarūs (Chote Bawa) travelled at his side.

His passing aboard the ship

He did not complete the voyage. Somewhere upon the open sea, Kale Bawa passed from this world on 8 Rabīʿ al-Ākhir 1368 AH — 8 February 1949.

Under ordinary maritime practice, his body should have been committed to the Indian Ocean. But the captain of the ship, having witnessed the spiritual rank of Kale Bawa in a dream, decided against it. He ordered an unscheduled change of course and brought the ship into the port of Mombasa, so that Kale Bawa could receive a complete and proper Islamic burial on land.

His resting place

Cutchi Sunni Muslims Jamat compound
Muyaka Road, Mombasa

Wife & children

His family

His wife was Sayyida Shamsunisa Begum. Of his children, several died young and were buried in Surat: Sayyid Zainul Abedien (about nineteen), Sayyid Husain (about eleven), and an infant son, Sayyid Alvi, who lived only some fifteen days. Three children survived into adulthood:

  • Hazrat Sayyid Naṣīruddīn al-ʿAydarūs — 1928–2001
  • Sayeda Fatima Begum — born 1935
  • Sayeda Salima Begum — born 1941

His son and most prominent successor, Naṣīruddīn — "Naseer Bawa" — received khilāfat directly from his father in thirty-three silsilas, as well as spiritual authorisation from his uncles. From 1974 onward he made many journeys to South Africa, leaving devoted followers in India, the United Kingdom, South Africa and Botswana. He was buried at Bansda, beside Hazrat Nādir Shāh Bawa.

Chote Bawa

Kale Bawa's younger brother, Chote Bawa, was a deeply learned religious scholar who also held an M.A. from Aligarh. He settled in Zeerust, South Africa, in 1932, serving as a school principal and teaching in Arabic, Urdu, Gujarati and English. He was an important spiritual figure with a magnetic impact on the community, including non-Muslims. He performed the Hajj and remained three years as a guest of the King, and the doors of the Kaaba were opened for him on two occasions.

The khilāfat

His authorised successors

Through these khalīfas, Kale Bawa's spiritual influence continued in India and Southern Africa:

  • Hazrat Sayyid Naṣīruddīn al-ʿAydarūs
  • Hazrat Sayyid Niẓāmuddīn al-Wāsiṭī Ṣāḥib
  • Janab Goolam Rasool Bapoo
  • Janab Goolam Mustafa Bapoo
  • Janab Haffejee Vaid Sahib

Legacy

A life of inheritance and responsibility

Kale Bawa's life united spiritual inheritance with public responsibility: a descendant of the Bā ʿAlawī sayyid family of Hadramawt, a member of the leading scholarly and saintly family of Surat, a Chishti spiritual master, a scholar, an organiser of Muslim political opinion, a Congress leader in India's struggle against British rule, and a teacher whose influence reached Botswana and South Africa.

He defended Muslim religious concerns while working for wider national cooperation. He supported resistance to British rule while urging discipline, peace and restraint. He challenged Congress when he believed Muslim participation, religious sensitivities or communal fairness were being neglected. His spirituality was inseparable from service and public responsibility: he urged his followers to pursue education, cleanliness, peace, moral discipline and care for others. That influence continued through his children, his authorised successors, and the communities he guided in India and Southern Africa.

Silsila · سلسلة

The chain of lineage

Forty generations, from the Messenger of God ﷺ to Kalebawa.

    رضي الله عنهم

    Haqīqat-e-dil ba ahl-e-dil ast,
    rabt-e-rūḥ ba nūr-e-shams ast

    The heart's truth is known by those of heart;
    the soul is bound to the light of the sun

    Ṣuhbat ba sālikān bahisht-e-rūḥ ast,
    ḥarf-e-murshid guhar-e-koh ast

    The company of seekers is a paradise for the soul;
    the words of the guide are jewels of the mountain

    Az qurbat-e-jānān gham-e-firāq nīst,
    ḥarf-e-rabt dil ast, faṣl-o-firāq nīst

    In closeness to the Beloved there is no grief of separation;
    the bond of hearts is not severed by distance or parting

    السيّد أحمد شهاب الدِّين العَيْدَروس

    Hazrat Khwāja Shah Sayyid Ahmad Shihabuddin al-ʿAydarūs

    Kale Bawa  ·  1881 — 1949  ·  Surat  →  Mombasa

    رحمه الله
